8.1.title: Board Tone-at-the-Top on Customer Information Protection
8.1.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will assess whether the board has demonstrably set the tone-at-the-top on customer information protection, looking beyond generic policy statements to evidence of active board championship. Examiners will scrutinise board communications, strategy documents, and annual reports for explicit references to customer information protection as a strategic priority. They will check whether the board has allocated agenda time specifically to customer information matters and whether board members can articulate the FSP's exposure and risk posture. A board that treats customer information protection as purely an IT or compliance matter rather than a board-level strategic concern will be flagged.

evidenceItems.name: Board Resolution on Customer Information Protection Strategy
evidenceItems.description: Formal board resolution or board paper demonstrating the board's commitment to safeguarding customer information, including articulation of potential consequences of breaches.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board resolution explicitly references MCIPD requirements and sets expectations for customer information protection
Board minutes show substantive discussion of customer information risks and consequences, not merely a rubber-stamp approval
The resolution is communicated organisation-wide as evidence of tone-at-the-top
Annual review cycle is documented with board calendar entries
commonGaps: No specific board resolution exists; customer information protection is assumed to be covered by general IT governance
Board minutes show only a brief note that policies were approved with no evidence of substantive discussion
Tone-at-the-top messaging exists only in internal memos from management, not directly from the board
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat - board resolution register and minutes
Corporate Communications - board-endorsed communications on customer information protection
Annual report - governance disclosures

auditTips: AI Generated: Request the last two years of board minutes and search for agenda items specifically addressing customer information protection - examiners will distinguish between dedicated agenda items and brief mentions buried in other reports.

AI Generated: Ask board members directly during interviews whether they can describe the FSP's customer information risk exposure and what consequences a breach could bring - inability to articulate this indicates tone-at-the-top has not been genuinely set.

8.2.title: Board Approval of Customer Information Protection Policies
8.2.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that the board has formally approved specific customer information protection policies, not merely noted them. Examiners will look for a board resolution with a date, resolution number, and signatures or recorded assent. They will test whether the approved policies are comprehensive - covering confidentiality and security - and whether the board satisfied itself that corresponding procedures and controls exist. A key red flag is delegation of approval to a management committee without board ratification.

evidenceItems.name: Board-Approved Customer Information Protection Policy
evidenceItems.description: The written policy document approved by the full board, covering confidentiality and security of customer information.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy bears a board resolution number, approval date, and evidence of full board approval (not just committee endorsement)
Policy scope explicitly covers both confidentiality and security of customer information
Version history shows structured review cycle with board re-approval at each revision
Policy references specific procedures and controls that implement its requirements
commonGaps: Policy was approved by the IT Steering Committee or Risk Committee but never formally ratified by the full board
Policy covers information security generally but does not specifically address customer information confidentiality
No version control or evidence of periodic board re-approval after initial approval
Policy exists but corresponding procedures and controls are not documented or referenced
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat - board papers and resolution register
Compliance department - policy library with version control
Document management system - policy repository

auditTips: AI Generated: Verify the policy approval chain: check that the full board (not just a committee) approved the policy. Cross-reference the board resolution register with the policy version history.

AI Generated: Test completeness by checking whether the policy addresses both confidentiality AND security - many FSPs focus on security controls but neglect confidentiality obligations under banking secrecy laws.

8.3.title: Board Oversight of Policy Implementation and Review
8.3.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will focus on whether the board receives and actively reviews reports from senior management on customer information management. Examiners will look for reports that include risk assessments, findings, and mitigation plans - not merely status dashboards. They will check board minutes for evidence that the board challenged management on report content and was satisfied that policies, procedures, and controls are adequate and effective. Periodic review to reflect changes in the operating environment is a specific examiner focus area.

evidenceItems.name: Senior Management Reports to Board on Customer Information Management
evidenceItems.description: Periodic reports from senior management to the board covering risk assessments, findings, and mitigation plans related to customer information protection.
whatGoodLooksLike: Reports are submitted on a defined schedule (at least quarterly) and are part of the standing board agenda
Each report includes risk assessment findings, control effectiveness evaluation, and status of mitigation plans
Board minutes record substantive discussion and challenge on report content, not merely noting receipt
Reports are updated to reflect changes in the operating environment and evolving risks
commonGaps: Reports are operational dashboards with KPIs but lack risk assessments and mitigation plans
Reports are presented to a management committee but not escalated to the board
Board minutes show reports were noted but no evidence of discussion or challenge
Reports have not been updated to reflect material changes such as new digital channels or remote working arrangements
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat - board papers archive
Senior management - periodic reports on customer information management
Risk management department - risk assessment reports
evidenceItems.format: PDF board papers with supporting data

auditTips: AI Generated: Request the last four quarters of board reports on customer information and check whether they contain all three required elements: risk assessments, findings, and mitigation plans.

AI Generated: Cross-reference report content with known changes in the operating environment (e.g., new digital products, remote working) to verify reports have been updated to reflect evolving risks.

8.4.title: Annual Written Assurance from Senior Management
8.4.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will specifically request the annual written assurance from senior management to the board. This is a binary compliance check - it either exists or it does not. Examiners will verify that the assurance covers both: (a) that customer information protection controls are working effectively, and (b) that outsourced service providers are fulfilling their contractual obligations. Examiners will also check whether the assurance is based on substantive assessment (testing, reviews, audits) rather than a perfunctory sign-off.

evidenceItems.name: Annual Written Assurance Letter
evidenceItems.description: Formal annual written assurance from senior management to the board confirming that customer information protection controls are working effectively and OSPs are meeting contractual obligations.
whatGoodLooksLike: A standalone letter or formal report signed by the CEO or designated senior officer, addressed to the board
Explicitly covers both control effectiveness AND outsourced service provider compliance
Assurance is supported by references to specific assessments, testing, or review activities conducted during the year
Board minutes record receipt and deliberation on the assurance, including any qualifications or exceptions noted
Issued annually within a defined timeline (e.g., within Q1 of the following year)
commonGaps: No formal annual assurance letter exists; management reports are assumed to provide implicit assurance
Assurance covers control effectiveness but omits outsourced service provider obligations
Assurance is a one-line statement with no reference to supporting assessments or testing
Assurance has not been issued in the current or prior year
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat - annual assurance letter on file
CEO or designated officer - signed assurance letter
Internal audit - reports supporting the assurance
OSP management function - OSP assessment reports referenced in the assurance
evidenceItems.format: PDF signed letter with supporting assessment references

auditTips: AI Generated: This is a hard compliance requirement - ask for the annual written assurance letter immediately. If it does not exist, it is a clear finding.

AI Generated: Check whether the assurance explicitly mentions outsourced service providers and their contractual obligations - many FSPs provide a generic assurance on controls but forget the OSP component.

9.1-9.2.title: Senior Management Accountability and Designated Officer
9.1-9.2.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that senior management accountability for customer information protection is formally documented and that a designated officer of sufficient senior ranking has been appointed. Examiners will check the designated officer's job description, reporting lines, and whether they have the authority and resources to discharge their responsibilities. The designated officer must be responsible for communicating policies and coordinating with key stakeholders. Examiners will also assess whether accountability is genuinely placed with senior management or has been effectively delegated down without proper oversight.

evidenceItems.name: Senior Management Accountability Framework for Customer Information
evidenceItems.description: Documentation establishing senior management's responsibility and accountability for customer information protection systems and controls.
whatGoodLooksLike: RACI matrix or accountability framework clearly assigning customer information protection responsibilities to named senior management roles
Board-approved terms of reference for relevant management committees with customer information protection in their mandate
Senior management performance objectives include customer information protection metrics
Documented reporting lines from the designated officer to the board
commonGaps: Accountability is diffused across departments without clear senior management ownership
No formal accountability framework exists; responsibilities are assumed based on organisational convention
Senior management performance objectives do not include customer information protection
suggestedSources: HR department - job descriptions and performance frameworks for senior management
Board Secretariat - management committee terms of reference
Compliance department - RACI matrices

evidenceItems.name: Designated Officer Appointment and Terms of Reference
evidenceItems.description: Formal appointment letter or board resolution designating a senior officer with overall responsibility for MCIPD implementation, including their specific responsibilities for policy communication and stakeholder coordination.
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal appointment letter or board resolution naming the designated officer by role and individual
Terms of reference explicitly include: (a) communicating policies throughout the FSP, and (b) coordinating with key stakeholders
The designated officer is of sufficient senior ranking with direct access to senior management and the board
The role is mapped to an existing senior position such as CDO, CIO, or DPO per paragraph 9.3 guidance
commonGaps: No formal appointment exists; the role is informally assumed by a mid-level manager
The designated officer lacks sufficient seniority or authority to coordinate across business lines
Terms of reference do not explicitly cover policy communication and stakeholder coordination responsibilities
The appointment has not been updated following organisational changes
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat - appointment resolution
HR department - job description and reporting lines
Compliance department - MCIPD implementation records
evidenceItems.format: PDF appointment letter or board resolution

auditTips: AI Generated: Ask to meet the designated officer and verify they can describe their MCIPD responsibilities. Check their seniority level - a manager-level appointee in a large FSP may not have sufficient authority.
AI Generated: Request evidence that the designated officer has actually communicated policies and coordinated with stakeholders - look for email trails, meeting records, or policy distribution logs.

9.4-9.6.title: Business Line Responsibility and Staff Communication
9.4-9.6.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will check whether responsibility for customer information confidentiality and security is embedded across business and functional lines, not concentrated solely in a centralised function. They will also verify that senior management has communicated clearly to all staff, representatives, and agents about the importance of preserving customer information confidentiality. Examiners will look for evidence of structured communication campaigns, not just policy distribution.

evidenceItems.name: Business Line Responsibility Assignment
evidenceItems.description: Documentation assigning customer information protection responsibilities to business and functional lines, demonstrating decentralised accountability.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each business and functional line has a documented data steward or information protection champion
Business line operating procedures reference customer information handling requirements
Business line risk assessments explicitly address customer information risks
Regular reporting from business lines to the designated officer on customer information matters
commonGaps: Customer information protection is treated as solely the responsibility of IT or compliance
Business lines have no documented responsibilities for customer information protection
No mechanism exists for business lines to report customer information issues to the designated officer
suggestedSources: Business line heads - operating procedures and risk assessments
Designated officer - stakeholder coordination records
HR department - business line role descriptions

evidenceItems.name: Senior Management Communications on Customer Information Protection
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of clear messaging from senior management to staff, representatives, and agents on the importance of preserving customer information confidentiality and security.
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual or periodic communications from senior management (CEO, designated officer) emphasising customer information protection
Communications are distributed to all staff AND to representatives and agents, not just internal staff
Messaging addresses both confidentiality and security obligations
Communication reach and acknowledgement is tracked
commonGaps: No structured communications exist; reliance is placed on policy distribution alone
Communications reach internal staff but not representatives and agents
Messaging is generic and does not specifically address customer information obligations
suggestedSources: Corporate Communications - internal communication records
Designated officer - communication logs
Agent management team - agent communication records

auditTips: AI Generated: Interview business line heads and ask them to describe their specific responsibilities for customer information protection - if they defer entirely to IT or compliance, responsibility has not been effectively placed on business lines.
AI Generated: Sample representatives and agents to verify they received communications about customer information importance - this is frequently overlooked.

9.5.title: Representative and Agent Control Measures
9.5.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that appointed representatives and agents have adequate control measures in place for customer information protection. This extends the FSP's obligations to its distribution and agent network. Examiners will look for evidence of control requirements being imposed, monitored, and enforced across the agent and representative network.

evidenceItems.name: Representative and Agent Control Framework
evidenceItems.description: Documentation of control measures required of and implemented by appointed representatives and agents for safeguarding customer information.
whatGoodLooksLike: Written control requirements specified in representative and agent agreements
Periodic assessment or audit of representative and agent control measures
Evidence of remediation where control gaps at representatives or agents are identified
Control requirements are proportionate to the volume and sensitivity of customer information handled
commonGaps: Agent agreements mention confidentiality but do not specify control measures
No assessment or monitoring of whether agents have implemented required controls
Control requirements have not been updated since initial agent appointment
suggestedSources: Agent management team - agreements, assessments, and remediation records
Compliance department - agent oversight records
Internal audit - agent audit reports

auditTips: AI Generated: Sample a selection of representative and agent agreements and check for specific control measure requirements, not just generic confidentiality clauses.
AI Generated: Request evidence of any assessments or audits conducted on agents' customer information protection controls in the last two years.

9.7.title: Staff and Agent Training on Customer Information Policies
9.7.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that adequate training on customer information policies is provided to FSP staff AND that representatives and agents provide adequate training to their own staff. Examiners will check training records, completion rates, content relevance, and whether training extends to the entire representative and agent network. Adequacy will be assessed in terms of content, frequency, and coverage.

evidenceItems.name: Training Programme and Completion Records
evidenceItems.description: Training programme on customer information protection policies with records of completion for FSP staff and evidence that representatives/agents also train their staff.
whatGoodLooksLike: Structured training programme covering MCIPD-relevant policies, updated at least annually
Training completion records showing high completion rates (above 90%) across all staff levels
Training content covers specific MCIPD requirements, not just generic data protection
Contractual requirements for representatives and agents to train their own staff, with evidence of compliance
commonGaps: Training is limited to an annual e-learning module with no assessment of understanding
Training records are incomplete or do not cover all staff categories
No mechanism exists to verify that representatives and agents have trained their own staff
Training content has not been updated to reflect current MCIPD requirements
suggestedSources: Learning management system - training records and completion data
HR department - training programme documentation
Agent management team - agent training compliance records

auditTips: AI Generated: Pull training completion data for the last 12 months and check for gaps in coverage across departments and staff levels. Pay particular attention to contract staff and new joiners.
AI Generated: Ask how the FSP verifies that representatives and agents are training their own staff - many FSPs impose the contractual requirement but never verify compliance.

9.8.title: Independent Review (Biennial)
9.8.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will check whether an independent review has been carried out at least once every two years, in accordance with paragraphs 10.53-10.56. This is a hard compliance requirement with a specific frequency. Examiners will verify independence of the reviewer, scope of the review, and whether findings have been acted upon.

evidenceItems.name: Independent Review Report and Schedule
evidenceItems.description: The independent review report on effectiveness of customer information protection policies, procedures, and controls, with evidence of the review schedule.
whatGoodLooksLike: Independent review completed within the required two-year cycle
Reviewer is independent of business units handling customer information (e.g., internal audit)
Review scope covers effectiveness of policies, procedures, AND control measures
Written findings communicated to both senior management and the board
Remedial actions tracked to completion
commonGaps: No independent review has been conducted, or the two-year cycle has lapsed
Review was conducted by a function not independent of the business units
Review scope was limited to IT controls and did not cover policies and procedures holistically
Findings were communicated to management but not to the board
suggestedSources: Internal audit department - review reports and work programme
Board Secretariat - board papers presenting review findings
Compliance department - remediation tracking

auditTips: AI Generated: Check the date of the last independent review and verify it falls within the two-year window. If it is overdue, this is an immediate compliance finding.

AI Generated: Verify reviewer independence - the review function must be independent of business units that handle customer information. Internal audit qualifies; a compliance team within the same division may not.

9.9-9.10.title: Board Notification and Reporting on Breaches
9.9-9.10.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that senior management notifies the board upon detection of customer information breaches, with urgency proportional to breach severity and data sensitivity. They will also check that investigation findings are reported to the board per paragraph 11.21. Examiners will compare the breach register against board minutes to verify that all notifiable breaches reached the board.

evidenceItems.name: Board Breach Notification Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of senior management notifications to the board regarding detected customer information breaches, including investigation findings.
whatGoodLooksLike: Board minutes record notification of all material breaches with appropriate urgency
Notifications include breach nature, sensitivity of data involved, and initial assessment
Investigation findings are subsequently reported to the board per paragraph 11.21
Board actions and directives in response to breach notifications are documented
commonGaps: Minor breaches are not escalated to the board even where data sensitivity warrants it
Breach notifications are batched into periodic reports rather than provided promptly
Investigation findings are reported to management committees but not to the board
No criteria exist for determining which breaches require board notification
suggestedSources: Board Secretariat - board minutes and papers
Designated officer - breach notification records
Breach register - cross-referenced with board reporting
evidenceItems.format: PDF board minutes and breach notification records

auditTips: AI Generated: Cross-reference the breach register against board minutes for the same period to verify all material breaches were escalated to the board.
AI Generated: Check whether breach notification criteria are documented - without defined criteria, the FSP may be inconsistent in what reaches the board.

10.1-10.4.title: Customer Information Risk Assessment
10.1-10.4.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that FSPs have conducted a comprehensive risk assessment covering threats and vulnerabilities to customer information, including likelihood-and-impact assessments. The assessment must cover third-party service providers and outsourcing arrangements and be proportionate to the FSP's size, nature, complexity, and sensitivity of customer information held. Examiners will look for mitigation plans that are developed and implemented based on identified threats. A risk assessment that is copied from a template without customisation to the FSP's specific environment will be flagged.

evidenceItems.name: Customer Information Risk Assessment Report
evidenceItems.description: Comprehensive risk assessment identifying threats and vulnerabilities to customer information, including likelihood-and-impact analysis and mitigation plans.
whatGoodLooksLike: Covers internal and external threats across the full customer information lifecycle (collection, storage, use, transmission, sharing, disposal)
Includes likelihood and impact assessment for each identified threat and vulnerability
Covers third-party service providers and outsourcing arrangements explicitly
Proportionate to FSP's size, complexity, and volume/sensitivity of customer information
Mitigation plans are developed for each identified risk with owners, timelines, and status tracking
Updated at least annually or following material changes in the operating environment
commonGaps: Risk assessment covers IT risks generally but does not specifically address customer information threats
Third-party and outsourcing risks are omitted from the assessment
Assessment is not proportionate - either overly complex for a small FSP or too superficial for a large one
Mitigation plans are identified but not implemented or tracked
Assessment has not been updated following material changes such as new products, channels, or outsourcing arrangements
suggestedSources: Risk management department - risk assessment reports and registers
IT security team - threat and vulnerability assessments
Compliance department - third-party risk assessments
Internal audit - risk assessment working papers

auditTips: AI Generated: Check whether the risk assessment specifically identifies customer information threats or merely recycles a generic IT risk assessment. The MCIPD requires customer-information-specific threat identification.

AI Generated: Verify proportionality - a small takaful operator should not have the same risk assessment framework as a large bank. Conversely, a large bank with millions of customer records should not have a cursory risk assessment.
AI Generated: Test whether mitigation plans have actually been implemented by sampling 3-5 high-rated risks and asking for evidence of the stated mitigation actions.

10.6-10.11.title: Customer Information Protection Policies and Procedures
10.6-10.11.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will conduct a detailed review of the FSP's written policies and procedures to verify they cover the full customer information lifecycle (collection, access, storage, use, transmission, sharing, disclosure, and disposal). Specific policies must exist for: (a) off-site work arrangements, (b) physical document handling and transport, (c) portable IT equipment and storage devices, and (d) breach incident handling. Examiners will check that policies are proportionate, accessible to staff, clearly communicated by the designated officer, and continually reviewed. Outdated policies that do not reflect the current operating environment are a common finding.

evidenceItems.name: Suite of Customer Information Protection Policies and Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Complete set of written policies and procedures covering the full lifecycle of customer information, including the four mandatory specific policy areas.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policies cover all lifecycle stages: collection, access, storage, use, transmission, sharing, disclosure, and disposal
Specific policies exist for: (a) off-site work, (b) physical document handling/transport, (c) portable IT equipment/storage, (d) breach incident handling
Policies are proportionate to the FSP's size, nature, complexity, and data sensitivity
Clear procedures translate policy requirements into operational steps
Version control with documented review and approval history
commonGaps: Policies exist for IT security but do not specifically address customer information lifecycle stages
One or more of the four mandatory specific policy areas (off-site work, physical documents, portable devices, breach handling) is missing
Policies are disproportionate - either overly generic or unnecessarily complex for the FSP's operations
Procedures do not exist or are not aligned to policy statements
Policies have not been reviewed or updated for more than 12 months
suggestedSources: Compliance department - policy register and library
Document management system - policy repository with version control
Designated officer - policy communication records
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX suite of policies with version control

evidenceItems.name: Policy Communication and Accessibility Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that policies and procedures are readily accessible to staff and clearly communicated by the designated officer.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policies are published on an accessible intranet or document management system
Communication from the designated officer evidencing policy distribution and awareness campaigns
Staff acknowledgement records confirming receipt and understanding of policies
Policy accessibility is verified through periodic checks or surveys
commonGaps: Policies are stored in a shared drive that not all staff can access
No evidence that the designated officer has communicated policies
Staff acknowledgement is not tracked or has low completion rates
Policies are communicated only at onboarding, with no periodic refresher
suggestedSources: Designated officer - communication records
Intranet/document management system - access logs and publication records
HR department - staff acknowledgement records
evidenceItems.format: Intranet records, email logs, acknowledgement reports

evidenceItems.name: Policy Review Records and Change Log
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of continual policy review including review schedules, change logs, and rationale for updates.
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented review schedule with evidence reviews are conducted on time
Change log recording what was changed, why, and when for each policy revision
Reviews triggered by changes in operating environment (not just calendar-driven)
Board or senior management re-approval following material policy changes
commonGaps: No documented review schedule exists; reviews are ad hoc
Policies show a review date but no change log evidencing what was considered or changed
Reviews are purely calendar-driven and do not respond to environmental changes
Policies were last reviewed more than 18 months ago
suggestedSources: Compliance department - policy review schedule and change logs
Document management system - version history
Board Secretariat - approval records for policy changes
evidenceItems.format: PDF change logs and review records

auditTips: AI Generated: Create a checklist of the eight lifecycle stages (collection, access, storage, use, transmission, sharing, disclosure, disposal) and the four mandatory specific policies (off-site work, physical documents, portable devices, breach handling). Map each policy document to these requirements and identify gaps.

AI Generated: Test accessibility by asking a randomly selected frontline staff member to locate the customer information protection policy - if they cannot find it within a few minutes, accessibility is inadequate.
AI Generated: Check whether policies have been updated to reflect post-pandemic remote working arrangements and new digital channels.

10.12-10.24.title: ICT Preventive and Detective Controls
10.12-10.24.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will assess the adequacy of both preventive and detective ICT controls for customer information protection. Key focus areas include: (1) off-site work controls including authentication and encryption, (2) portable storage device controls with business-need restrictions, (3) call recording access on need-to-know basis, (4) deterrent mechanisms against unauthorised disclosure, (5) web-based communication and social media restrictions for staff handling customer information, and (6) detection mechanisms for unauthorised access, unusual viewing, suspicious downloads, and unauthorised disclosure. Examiners will test whether controls are regularly monitored and remain responsive to changing threats.

evidenceItems.name: ICT Controls Inventory and Effectiveness Assessment
evidenceItems.description: Inventory of preventive and detective ICT controls deployed to protect customer information, with evidence of regular effectiveness monitoring.
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive inventory of controls mapped to specific customer information threats
Both preventive (access controls, encryption, DLP) and detective (monitoring, alerting, audit trail) controls are documented
Regular monitoring reports showing control effectiveness metrics
Controls are updated in response to changing threats
commonGaps: Controls are primarily preventive with weak detective capabilities
No regular monitoring of control effectiveness
Controls have not been updated to address new threats such as AI-assisted data extraction
Control inventory is incomplete or not mapped to customer information risks
suggestedSources: IT security team - control inventory and monitoring reports
SOC - detection and monitoring capabilities
GRC platform - control register

evidenceItems.name: Off-Site Work Controls and Remote Access Security
evidenceItems.description: Documentation and evidence of controls for off-site work arrangements, including ICT equipment controls, authentication, and encryption.
whatGoodLooksLike: Off-site work policy with specific controls for remote access to customer information
Multi-factor authentication enforced for all remote access
Data encryption on ICT equipment used off-site
Secure data transmission protocols enforced
Controls extend to staff, representatives, and agents undertaking off-site work
commonGaps: Off-site work policy exists but specific controls for customer information access are not defined
MFA is not enforced for all remote access scenarios
Encryption is not enforced on all off-site devices
Controls do not extend to representatives and agents
suggestedSources: IT security team - remote access configuration and logs
MDM platform - device encryption and compliance reports
HR department - remote work policy

evidenceItems.name: Portable Storage Device Controls
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of controls restricting customer information downloads to portable storage devices to staff with legitimate business need, with data protection measures.
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy restricting portable storage device use to authorised staff with documented business justification
Technical controls (USB port management, device encryption) enforced
Register of authorised portable storage devices and their users
Periodic review of authorisation list
commonGaps: USB ports are not managed or blocked by default
No register of authorised portable devices exists
Business need justification is not documented for authorised users
Encryption is not enforced on portable storage devices
suggestedSources: IT security team - endpoint management platform
DLP tools - portable device usage logs
Business units - business justification records

evidenceItems.name: Deterrent Mechanisms Against Unauthorised Disclosure
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of mechanisms creating a strong deterrent effect against unauthorised disclosure of customer information by staff.
whatGoodLooksLike: Documented disciplinary framework with clear consequences for unauthorised disclosure
Staff awareness of disciplinary actions evidenced through training and communications
Physical deterrents such as CCTV in high-risk areas, open office concepts
Personal electronic device restrictions in high-risk areas (data centres, call centres, dealing rooms)
Whistleblowing mechanisms for reporting suspected unauthorised disclosure
commonGaps: Disciplinary framework exists in HR policies but staff are not specifically aware of consequences for data disclosure
No physical deterrents in high-risk areas
Personal device restrictions are not enforced in call centres or data centres
No whistleblowing mechanism specific to customer information concerns
suggestedSources: HR department - disciplinary policy and records
Facilities management - CCTV and physical security records
Compliance department - whistleblowing programme
Security team - device restriction enforcement records

evidenceItems.name: Web-Based Communication and Social Media Restrictions
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that web-based communication and social media platforms, particularly end-to-end encrypted platforms, are restricted for staff handling customer information.
whatGoodLooksLike: Web filtering policy explicitly restricts encrypted messaging platforms (WhatsApp Desktop, Facebook Messenger, Skype) for staff handling customer information
Technical controls (web proxy, firewall rules) enforce the restrictions
Monitoring of compliance with restrictions
Exception process documented for any business-justified access
commonGaps: Web filtering is applied generically rather than targeted to staff handling customer information
WhatsApp Web and similar platforms are not blocked despite the specific MCIPD requirement
Restrictions are in policy but not technically enforced
No monitoring of compliance with social media restrictions
suggestedSources: IT security team - web proxy and firewall configuration
DLP tools - web communication monitoring logs
HR department - acceptable use policy
evidenceItems.format: Technical configuration exports, PDF monitoring reports

evidenceItems.name: Detection Mechanisms for Unauthorised Activity
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of detection mechanisms for: (a) unauthorised access, (b) unusual frequent viewing, (c) suspicious downloading, and (d) unauthorised disclosure to external parties.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated detection rules configured for all four categories of unauthorised activity
Regular review of audit trails and detection alerts
Random periodic sample checks conducted and documented
Detection mechanisms generate actionable alerts with defined escalation procedures
Detection capabilities are tested periodically for effectiveness
commonGaps: Detection mechanisms cover unauthorised access but not unusual viewing or suspicious downloads
Audit trails exist but are not regularly reviewed
No random sample checks are conducted
Detection alerts are generated but not acted upon due to alert fatigue
Detection mechanisms for unauthorised disclosure to external parties are absent
suggestedSources: SOC or IT security team - SIEM rules and alert logs
DLP platform - detection rule configuration and incident logs
Internal audit - sample check records
Application teams - user activity monitoring logs
evidenceItems.format: SIEM/DLP reports, PDF audit trail reviews

auditTips: AI Generated: Map each ICT control to the specific MCIPD paragraph it addresses - examiners will check for coverage across all mandatory requirements, not just whether some controls exist.

AI Generated: Test detection controls by requesting recent alert samples and following the trail from detection to investigation to resolution. Alerts that were generated but never investigated are a significant finding.
AI Generated: Specifically check whether WhatsApp Desktop and similar encrypted messaging platforms are blocked for staff in customer-facing roles - this is a frequently missed MCIPD requirement.
AI Generated: Verify that call recording access controls are enforced on a need-to-know basis (10.18) - request the access list and compare it to staff whose roles require access.

10.25-10.27.title: Access Controls and Data Mapping
10.25-10.27.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that: (1) role profiles include descriptions of access rights to customer information, (2) customer information locations across all systems are identified with multi-level access controls (application, database, OS, network), and (3) access rights are regularly reviewed with immediate revocation for departing or role-changing staff. Examiners commonly test access revocation timeliness by sampling recent leavers and checking when their access was revoked.

evidenceItems.name: Role Profiles with Access Rights Descriptions
evidenceItems.description: Job role profiles that include descriptions of customer information access rights for each relevant role.
whatGoodLooksLike: Each role profile includes a section defining what customer information the role can access and at what level
Access rights descriptions are specific (e.g., 'read-only access to customer account details') not generic ('system access')
Role profiles are reviewed when roles change or new systems are introduced
RBAC implementation aligns with documented role profiles
commonGaps: Role profiles exist but do not include customer information access rights descriptions
Access rights are defined in IT systems but not reflected in HR role profiles
Role profiles are outdated and do not reflect current system access
Generic access descriptions that do not differentiate between types of customer information
suggestedSources: HR department - role profiles and job descriptions
IAM team - RBAC matrix and access rights definitions
Business line managers - role-specific access requirements
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX role profiles, RBAC matrix spreadsheet

evidenceItems.name: Customer Information Data Map and Multi-Level Access Controls
evidenceItems.description: Data map identifying the location of customer information across systems, with evidence of access controls at application, database, operating system, and network levels.
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive data map showing all systems containing customer information
Access controls documented at all four levels: application, database, OS, and network
Controls are designed to prevent unauthorised access, modification, and disclosure
Data map is maintained and updated when new systems or data stores are introduced
commonGaps: No formal data map exists; customer information locations are known informally but not documented
Access controls exist at application level but not at database or OS level
Data map is incomplete, omitting shadow IT, legacy systems, or unstructured data stores
Data map has not been updated to include new systems or cloud services
suggestedSources: IT architecture team - system inventory and data flow diagrams
IAM team - access control configurations across system layers
Data governance team - data inventory or data catalogue
Cloud operations - cloud-hosted data access controls
evidenceItems.format: Data map (Visio/PDF), access control configuration reports

evidenceItems.name: Access Review and Revocation Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of regular access rights reviews and immediate revocation of access for departing staff or staff changing roles.
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular access reviews (at least quarterly for privileged access, semi-annually for all) with documented results
Immediate access revocation on staff departure, evidenced by comparing HR departure dates with access revocation timestamps
Access changes on role transfers processed promptly with removal of access no longer required
Exceptions are documented and approved by management
commonGaps: Access reviews are infrequent or limited to a subset of systems
Delay between staff departure and access revocation (often days or weeks rather than immediate)
Role transfers result in access accumulation - new access is granted but old access is not revoked
No documented access review process or records
suggestedSources: IAM team - access review reports and revocation logs
HR department - leaver and role change data
Active Directory or IAM platform - account disable/delete logs
Internal audit - access review working papers

auditTips: AI Generated: Sample 10 recent leavers from HR data and check the gap between their last working day and the date their system access was revoked. Any gap exceeding one working day is a finding under clause 10.27.
AI Generated: Request the data map and randomly select three systems to verify that access controls are actually implemented at all four levels (application, database, OS, network).
AI Generated: Check for role-access accumulation by sampling staff who have transferred between departments and verifying that their previous role's access was revoked.

10.28-10.30.title: Physical Security Controls
10.28-10.30.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will assess physical security controls for both paper and electronic customer information. They will inspect areas storing large amounts of or sensitive customer information (server rooms, filing rooms) for access restrictions and intruder deterrents. Examiners will conduct site walkthroughs to verify controls are implemented in practice, not just documented.

evidenceItems.name: Physical Security Controls for Customer Information
evidenceItems.description: Documentation and evidence of physical security controls protecting customer information in paper and electronic forms.
whatGoodLooksLike: Physical access controls (card readers, biometrics) at server rooms, filing rooms, and data processing areas
CCTV coverage of sensitive areas with adequate retention of recordings
Intruder deterrents (alarms, security guards) at critical information storage areas
Access logs maintained for all restricted areas
Regular physical security assessments or walkthroughs documented
commonGaps: Physical security focuses on data centres but neglects branch filing rooms or record storage areas
Access logs for restricted areas are not regularly reviewed
CCTV coverage gaps in critical areas
Physical security controls for paper records are weaker than for electronic systems
Clear-desk policy is not implemented despite guidance in paragraph 10.30
suggestedSources: Facilities management - physical access control system reports and CCTV records
Security team - physical security assessment reports
Branch operations - branch physical security records
Internal audit - physical security review findings
evidenceItems.format: PDF/access control system reports, CCTV coverage maps

auditTips: AI Generated: Conduct a physical walkthrough of at least one server room, one filing room, and one branch to verify physical security controls are implemented as documented.
AI Generated: Check whether paper records containing customer information are secured - many FSPs focus on electronic security but have unsecured filing cabinets in open areas.
AI Generated: Verify CCTV coverage of areas where customer information is stored and check retention periods of recordings.

10.31.subsection: C: Control measures - Off-site and data-in-transit
10.31.title: Off-Site Collection and Data-in-Transit Controls
10.31.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that clear policies and procedures with adequate controls exist for customer information collected off-site and in-transit. This covers scenarios such as agents collecting customer applications at customer premises, courier services for physical documents, and electronic transmission of customer information. Examiners will check both physical document security and portable device protection.

evidenceItems.name: Off-Site Collection and Data-in-Transit Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedures and controls for handling customer information collected off-site and in-transit.
whatGoodLooksLike: Specific procedures for agents and representatives collecting customer information at customer premises
Secure storage requirements for physical documents during transit
Encryption requirements for customer information on portable devices during transit
Chain of custody procedures for physical documents
Regular compliance checks on off-site collection processes
commonGaps: No specific procedures exist for off-site collection - agents use ad hoc methods
Physical documents are transported in unsecured bags or folders
Portable devices used off-site are not encrypted
No chain of custody for physical documents in transit
suggestedSources: Agent management team - off-site collection procedures
Operations team - document handling and transit procedures
IT security - portable device encryption reports

auditTips: AI Generated: Interview agents or representatives who collect customer information off-site and ask them to walk through their process - compare observed practices with documented procedures.
AI Generated: Check physical document transit methods - are forms and copies carried in lockable bags or secured containers, or in open folders and personal bags?

10.32-10.37.title: Customer Information Disposal and Destruction
10.32-10.37.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that proper disposal procedures exist covering identification of data no longer needed, appropriate secure destruction methods, and outsourced destruction controls. Key areas include: procedures for identifying no-longer-needed data, secure destruction methods (not just file deletion), risk-benefit assessment for outsourced destruction, secure storage before collection (tamper-proof bags or locked containers), and random checks on outsourced destruction providers. Simple file deletion or reformatting is explicitly insufficient per the guidance.

evidenceItems.name: Data Disposal and Destruction Procedures and Records
evidenceItems.description: Procedures for identifying and securely disposing of customer information no longer required, with records of disposal activities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Written procedures for identifying data no longer required operationally or legally
Defined retention periods aligned with legal requirements
Industry-standard destruction methods (data wiping software, physical destruction, degaussing) documented and applied
Certificates of destruction obtained and retained
For outsourced destruction: risk-benefit assessment, secure pre-collection storage (tamper-proof bags or locked containers), and random verification checks
commonGaps: No defined process for identifying data that is no longer required
Reliance on simple file deletion or reformatting rather than secure destruction
No certificates of destruction obtained from outsourced providers
Documents awaiting destruction are stored insecurely before collection
No random checks conducted on outsourced destruction providers
suggestedSources: Records management team - disposal procedures and certificates of destruction
IT operations - media sanitisation records
Procurement/vendor management - outsourced destruction contracts and assessment records
Facilities management - secure destruction bin and collection records
evidenceItems.format: PDF procedures, certificates of destruction, inspection reports

auditTips: AI Generated: Ask to see the secure destruction bins or locked containers used before outsourced collection - check whether they are tamper-proof as required by paragraph 10.36.
AI Generated: Request records of random checks conducted on outsourced destruction providers (10.37) - many FSPs have never conducted such checks despite the mandatory requirement.
AI Generated: Verify that digital device disposal includes proper sanitisation, not just reformatting - request evidence of data wiping or physical destruction records.

10.39-10.52.subsection: D: Staff, Representatives, Agents and External Vendors' Personnel
10.39-10.52.title: Staff Controls, Training, and Accountability
10.39-10.52.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will assess the full spectrum of staff-related controls: (1) confidentiality undertakings in employment contracts, (2) external vendor personnel vetting and monitoring, (3) staff awareness programmes, (4) compliance monitoring mechanisms, (5) training programmes including onboarding, (6) training effectiveness measurement, (7) investigation and disciplinary processes for breaches, and (8) accountability for representatives and agents. Examiners frequently sample employment contracts and onboarding records to verify confidentiality undertaking compliance.

evidenceItems.name: Employment Contract Confidentiality Undertaking
evidenceItems.description: Sample employment contracts and confidentiality undertakings specifying legal obligations to safeguard customer information and consequences for non-compliance.
whatGoodLooksLike: Confidentiality undertaking is a mandatory component of all employment contracts
Undertaking specifies obligations under relevant written law (FSA, IFSA, DFIA as applicable)
Clear articulation of consequences for non-compliance
100% signing rate evidenced through HR records
Undertaking applies to all staff categories including contract and temporary staff
commonGaps: Confidentiality clause exists in employment contracts but does not reference specific legal obligations under banking/financial services law
Legacy staff hired before MCIPD have not signed updated undertakings
Contract and temporary staff are not covered
Consequences for non-compliance are vague or not specified
suggestedSources: HR department - employment contract templates and signing records
Legal department - confidentiality undertaking template
Compliance department - compliance with signing requirements
evidenceItems.format: PDF/DOCX contract templates and signing records

evidenceItems.name: External Vendor Personnel Vetting and Monitoring
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that external vendors working on FSP premises (security guards, cleaners, maintenance) appropriately vet and monitor their personnel.
whatGoodLooksLike: Vendor contracts require vetting and monitoring of their personnel
FSP verifies vendor vetting practices through assessments or attestations
Access controls for vendor personnel in areas containing customer information
Vendor personnel are escorted in sensitive areas
commonGaps: No vetting requirement in vendor contracts for on-premises personnel
FSP does not verify whether vendors actually conduct vetting
Vendor personnel have unescorted access to areas containing customer information
Monitoring of vendor personnel behaviour is not conducted
suggestedSources: Procurement/vendor management - vendor contracts and vetting attestations
Facilities management - vendor personnel access records
Security team - escort and monitoring records
evidenceItems.format: PDF contracts, attestation letters, access logs

evidenceItems.name: Staff Awareness Programme and Records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of ongoing staff awareness programme on customer information protection, including awareness of confidentiality obligations, policy compliance requirements, and consequences of breach.
whatGoodLooksLike: Structured awareness programme with multiple touchpoints (not just annual training)
Covers all three required elements: (a) confidentiality importance, (b) policy compliance, (c) breach consequences
Regular reminders through communications, posters, intranet, team meetings
Awareness reaches all staff levels from senior management to frontline
New staff receive specific onboarding training on customer information protection (10.44-10.45)
commonGaps: Awareness programme is limited to annual e-learning with no ongoing reinforcement
Content is generic information security rather than specific to customer information obligations
New staff onboarding does not include specific customer information protection training
New staff are not notified of consequences for non-compliance (10.45)
No measurement of awareness levels or training effectiveness (10.47)
suggestedSources: Learning management system - training records and awareness campaign records
Corporate communications - awareness materials
HR department - onboarding programme documentation
Compliance department - awareness survey results
evidenceItems.format: LMS reports, communication records, survey results

evidenceItems.name: Training Effectiveness Measurement
evidenceItems.description: Mechanisms and records demonstrating measurement of training effectiveness on safeguarding customer information.
whatGoodLooksLike: Post-training assessments with pass/fail criteria
Annual awareness surveys assessing staff understanding
Survey results analysed and acted upon
Phishing simulation or scenario-based testing
Year-on-year trend analysis of awareness levels
commonGaps: Training completion is tracked but effectiveness is not measured
No awareness surveys or assessments are conducted
Survey results are collected but not analysed or acted upon
No mechanism to test whether staff apply training in practice
suggestedSources: Learning management system - assessment results
Compliance or HR department - awareness survey data and analysis
IT security team - phishing simulation results
evidenceItems.format: PDF reports, survey data, assessment results

evidenceItems.name: Staff Compliance Monitoring Mechanisms
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of robust monitoring mechanisms ensuring staff adhere to customer information protection policies, procedures, and controls.
whatGoodLooksLike: Automated monitoring of policy compliance (e.g., clean desk checks, email DLP, USB usage)
Regular compliance spot-checks or walkthroughs documented
Non-compliance incidents tracked and addressed
Monitoring covers all relevant policies and procedures, not just IT controls
Monitoring results reported to management
commonGaps: Monitoring is limited to IT system logs without operational compliance checks
Spot-checks are conducted informally with no documentation
Non-compliance is identified but not tracked or addressed systematically
No monitoring of paper-based information handling
suggestedSources: Compliance department - monitoring programme and results
IT security team - DLP and monitoring reports
Branch operations - compliance check records
Internal audit - compliance monitoring findings
evidenceItems.format: PDF monitoring reports, compliance check records

evidenceItems.name: Staff Breach Investigation and Disciplinary Records
evidenceItems.description: Records of investigations into staff involvement in customer information breaches and disciplinary actions taken, including cases where no action was taken with documented rationale.
whatGoodLooksLike: Thorough and timely investigations documented for each staff-related breach
Appropriate disciplinary actions taken that serve as a deterrent (10.50)
Where no action is taken, rationale is documented and approved by senior management
Investigation results and actions reported to the board (10.51)
Actions communicated (anonymised) to staff as deterrent
commonGaps: Investigations are conducted but not documented
No action taken against staff in breach without documented rationale approved by senior management
Disciplinary actions are not communicated to wider staff, reducing deterrent effect
Investigation results are not reported to the board
suggestedSources: HR department - disciplinary records
Compliance/designated officer - investigation reports
Board Secretariat - board reports on breach investigations
Legal department - investigation files

auditTips: AI Generated: Sample 10 employment contracts across different hiring years and staff categories to verify confidentiality undertakings are consistently present and adequately worded.
AI Generated: Check the onboarding programme documentation for specific customer information protection training content and evidence that new staff are informed of consequences for non-compliance.

AI Generated: Request training effectiveness data - if the FSP can only show completion rates but not assessment scores or awareness survey results, the effectiveness measurement requirement under 10.47 is not met.
AI Generated: For staff breaches, check whether the FSP can demonstrate that decisions not to take disciplinary action were documented and approved by senior management (10.50 requirement).

10.53-10.56.title: Independent Review of Customer Information Protection
10.53-10.56.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that an independent review is conducted at least biennially by a function independent of business units handling customer information (e.g., internal audit). The review must assess: (a) senior management effectiveness and oversight, and (b) adequacy and effectiveness of customer information protection measures. Written findings must be communicated to both senior management and the board, and senior management must take timely corrective actions. Examiners will check the review scope, methodology, independence of the reviewer, and whether findings are tracked to remediation.

evidenceItems.name: Independent Review Report
evidenceItems.description: The independent review report covering effectiveness of policies, procedures, and controls for safeguarding customer information, including assessment of senior management oversight.
whatGoodLooksLike: Review conducted by a function independent of business units handling customer information (e.g., internal audit)
Scope covers both senior management oversight effectiveness AND control adequacy/effectiveness
Report includes scope, methodologies used, findings, evidence obtained, and limitations (per footnote 12)
Written findings communicated to both senior management and the board
Completed within the two-year cycle requirement
commonGaps: Review was conducted by a function that is not independent of the business units
Scope covers IT controls but does not assess senior management oversight effectiveness
Findings communicated to management but not to the board
Review cycle has lapsed beyond two years
Report lacks methodology description, evidence references, or limitation disclosures
suggestedSources: Internal audit department - review reports
Board Secretariat - board papers presenting review findings
Risk management - remediation tracking

evidenceItems.name: Remediation Action Tracking for Independent Review Findings
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that senior management has taken appropriate and timely actions to rectify deficiencies identified in the independent review.
whatGoodLooksLike: Remediation action plan developed for each finding with owners and deadlines
Regular tracking of remediation progress
Senior management sign-off on completed remediation actions
Overdue actions escalated appropriately
Evidence of verification that remediation actions are effective
commonGaps: Remediation plan exists but actions are overdue without escalation
Actions are marked complete without evidence of verification
No remediation plan was developed for some findings
Senior management is not actively tracking remediation progress
suggestedSources: Internal audit - finding tracking system
Compliance department - remediation tracker
Senior management - remediation progress reports

auditTips: AI Generated: Check the independence of the reviewer - internal audit typically qualifies, but verify the auditors assigned did not have prior involvement in the business units being reviewed.
AI Generated: Verify the report was communicated to BOTH senior management AND the board - not just one or the other.
AI Generated: Track remediation actions from the most recent review and check completion status - open or overdue items from the prior review cycle are a significant examiner concern.

11.1-11.7.title: Breach Handling and Response Plan
11.1-11.7.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will review the breach handling and response plan for completeness and test whether it is operationally effective. The plan must cover both deliberate and non-deliberate breaches and include escalation procedures, clear responsibility lines for containment and remediation, and recurrence prevention. Examiners will check whether staff understand escalation procedures, whether a mechanism exists to identify breaches (including through customer complaints), and whether the FSP can demonstrate immediate containment actions. Impact assessment methodology must cover all five required factors: intent, data sensitivity, number affected, exposure recipients, and fraud likelihood.

evidenceItems.name: Customer Information Breach Handling and Response Plan
evidenceItems.description: The formal breach handling and response plan covering both deliberate and non-deliberate breaches, with escalation procedures and responsibility lines.
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive plan covering both deliberate (intentional disclosure, theft) and non-deliberate (accidental loss, misdirected emails) breaches
Clear escalation procedures with defined timeframes and decision points
Assigned responsibilities for containment, investigation, notification, and remediation
Impact assessment methodology covering all five required factors (11.7)
Plan has been tested through simulation or tabletop exercise
Cross-FSP breach notification procedure (11.6) included
commonGaps: Plan covers only deliberate breaches and not accidental or non-deliberate breaches
Escalation procedures lack timeframes or decision criteria
No clear responsibility assignment for containment and remediation
Impact assessment methodology does not cover all five required factors
Plan has never been tested through simulation or exercise
No procedure for notifying other FSPs when their customer data is affected
suggestedSources: Designated officer - breach response plan documentation
IT security team - incident response procedures
Compliance department - breach handling framework
Risk management - impact assessment methodology

evidenceItems.name: Breach Identification Mechanisms
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of mechanisms to identify customer information breaches, including those arising from customer complaints.
whatGoodLooksLike: Multiple breach detection channels: system monitoring, staff reporting, customer complaints, third-party notifications
Customer complaint handling process includes breach identification triage
Complaints are investigated promptly for potential breach implications
Detection mechanisms are tested periodically
commonGaps: Breach detection relies solely on IT monitoring without considering customer complaints as a breach source
Customer complaints about potential data misuse are not systematically triaged for breach implications
No defined process for staff to report suspected breaches
Third-party breach notifications are not systematically captured
suggestedSources: Customer complaint management team - complaint records with breach flags
SOC or IT security team - breach detection systems
Compliance department - staff reporting channels
Designated officer - breach identification records

auditTips: AI Generated: Conduct a tabletop walkthrough of the breach response plan with the designated officer - ask them to describe step-by-step what happens when different types of breaches are detected.
AI Generated: Test whether frontline staff know how to escalate a suspected breach - interview 3-5 frontline staff and check their understanding of escalation procedures.

AI Generated: Check whether the impact assessment methodology explicitly addresses all five factors in paragraph 11.7 - many plans cover only data type and number of customers but omit intent assessment and fraud likelihood.

11.8-11.17.title: BNM Breach Notification
11.8-11.17.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that the FSP has clear criteria and procedures for determining when BNM notification is required. The three triggers are: (a) reputational risk or public confidence threat, (b) significant harm to customers, and (c) significant scale (over 1,000 customers). Examiners will check that notification content meets the minimum requirements (breach description, data types, number affected, consequences, remedial measures) and that incomplete information is handled by phased notification. The designated senior officer must confirm significant harm assessments. Examiners will also verify law enforcement notification procedures for fraud or criminal activity.

evidenceItems.name: BNM Notification Procedure and Records
evidenceItems.description: Documented procedure for notifying BNM of customer information breaches, including criteria assessment and notification records.
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear procedure documenting the three notification triggers and assessment criteria
Notification template covering all mandatory content items (11.16)
Procedure for phased notification when complete information is unavailable (11.17)
Records of all BNM notifications made, including timestamps and content
Designated officer sign-off on significant harm assessments (11.13)
Procedure for law enforcement notification when fraud or criminal activity is involved (11.15)
commonGaps: Notification criteria are not clearly documented, leading to inconsistent assessment
Notification content does not cover all mandatory items
No procedure for phased notification when information is incomplete
Designated officer sign-off on significant harm assessment is not obtained or documented
Law enforcement notification procedure is absent
suggestedSources: Designated officer - notification procedures and records
Compliance department - BNM notification templates and logs
Legal department - law enforcement notification records
Board Secretariat - board reports on breach notifications

auditTips: AI Generated: Review all breach records for the past two years and check whether any breach that met the notification criteria was not notified to BNM - under-reporting is a critical examiner concern.
AI Generated: Verify that the 1,000-customer threshold for significant scale (11.11) is clearly documented in the FSP's procedures and that staff responsible for assessment understand it.
AI Generated: Check whether the designated officer has signed off on significant harm assessments for each notifiable breach - this is a specific mandatory requirement under 11.13.

11.18-11.24.subsection: C: Submission of detailed investigation report
11.18-11.24.title: Breach Investigation and Reporting
11.18-11.24.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will scrutinise the FSP's breach investigation process, including: competency and independence of the investigating party, three-month investigation timeline, Board tabling of the detailed report, one-working-day BNM submission after Board tabling for qualifying breaches, designated officer sign-off, maintenance of a breach register with root causes and lessons learnt, and seven-year retention of investigation reports. Examiners will request the breach register and sample investigation reports to verify compliance with all procedural requirements.

evidenceItems.name: Breach Investigation Reports
evidenceItems.description: Detailed investigation reports ascertaining root causes and remedial actions, conducted by a competent party with independent oversight.
whatGoodLooksLike: Investigation conducted by a competent party with relevant expertise
Independent oversight by a party not from the business unit where the breach occurred
Investigation completed within three months of breach detection
Report includes root cause analysis, remedial actions, and recurrence prevention measures
Report tabled to the Board and submitted to BNM (with Appendix 1) within one working day of Board tabling for qualifying breaches
Signed off by the designated senior officer (9.2)
commonGaps: Investigations exceed the three-month timeline without documented justification
Investigation is conducted by the business unit where the breach occurred without independent oversight
Root cause analysis is superficial, identifying symptoms rather than root causes
Report not tabled to the Board
BNM submission exceeds the one-working-day deadline after Board tabling
Designated officer sign-off is absent
suggestedSources: Designated officer - investigation reports and timeline records
Internal audit or investigation team - investigation files
Board Secretariat - Board papers showing report tabling
Compliance department - BNM submission records

evidenceItems.name: Breach Register
evidenceItems.description: Register recording all customer information breaches with root causes, remedial actions, and lessons learnt.
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive register covering all customer information breaches, not just notifiable ones
Each entry includes root cause, remedial actions taken, and lessons learnt
Register is actively maintained and regularly reviewed
Lessons learnt are used to improve policies, procedures, and controls
Register is retained and accessible for at least seven years
commonGaps: Register only captures notifiable breaches, omitting minor incidents
Root causes are not documented or are superficial
Lessons learnt column is blank or generic
Register is not regularly reviewed or analysed for trends
Register does not exist at all
suggestedSources: Designated officer - breach register
Compliance department - incident management system
IT security team - security incident records
evidenceItems.format: Spreadsheet or incident management system export

auditTips: AI Generated: Request the breach register and check for completeness - compare against other incident sources (SOC logs, customer complaints, HR disciplinary records) to identify potential unreported breaches.
AI Generated: For each investigation report, verify the three-month timeline by comparing breach detection date with investigation completion date.

AI Generated: Check that investigation reports for qualifying breaches (significant harm, significant scale, deliberate disclosure) were submitted to BNM within one working day of Board tabling - request BNM submission timestamps.

11.25-11.31.title: Customer Breach Notification
11.25-11.31.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that the FSP has procedures for notifying affected customers when breaches cause or are likely to cause significant harm. Notifications must be in plain language and include all five mandatory content items: breach description, potential consequences, protective steps for customers, FSP remedial measures, and contact details. Direct notification is required unless disproportionate effort applies. Examiners will check that notification timing does not cause further harm, that staff are trained to handle customer queries, and that the FSP has a procedure for cases where affected customers cannot initially be identified.

evidenceItems.name: Customer Notification Procedures and Templates
evidenceItems.description: Procedures and templates for notifying affected customers of breaches that cause or are likely to cause significant harm.
whatGoodLooksLike: Clear procedure linking significant harm assessment to customer notification trigger
Notification template covering all five mandatory content items (11.27)
Template written in plain language, drawing attention to protective steps
Procedure for direct notification with fallback to public announcement for disproportionate effort cases
Procedure for cases where affected customers cannot initially be identified (11.30)
Staff training records for handling customer breach queries (11.31)
commonGaps: No customer notification template or procedure exists
Template does not cover all five mandatory content items
Notification language is technical or legalistic rather than plain language
No procedure for handling cases where affected customers cannot be identified
Staff handling customer queries have not been trained on breach-related queries
suggestedSources: Designated officer - customer notification procedures and templates
Corporate communications - notification drafting guidelines
Customer service team - training records for breach query handling
Compliance department - notification records for past breaches

auditTips: AI Generated: Review any customer notifications sent for past breaches and check whether they contain all five mandatory items. If no breaches have required customer notification, review the template for completeness.
AI Generated: Test staff readiness by asking customer-facing staff how they would handle a call from an affected customer asking about a breach - lack of preparation is a finding under 11.31.
AI Generated: Check the notification timing procedure - the notification must be without undue delay after BNM notification, and any delay must not cause further harm to customers.

12.1-12.3.title: OSP Risk Monitoring and Due Diligence
12.1-12.3.auditorFocus: AI Generated: BNM examiners will verify that FSPs actively monitor risks from outsourced service providers handling customer information and conduct adequate due diligence before appointing OSPs. Examiners will check that OSP policies, procedures, and controls are comparable to the FSP's own standards. Due diligence must assess processing, storage, and disposal risks. Examiners will compare the FSP's own control standards with what it requires and verifies from its OSPs.

evidenceItems.name: OSP Risk Monitoring Framework
evidenceItems.description: Framework and evidence of ongoing monitoring of risks arising from outsourced service providers handling customer information.
whatGoodLooksLike: OSP risk register identifying all OSPs with access to customer information
Risk ratings assigned to each OSP based on volume and sensitivity of data accessed
Ongoing monitoring activities proportionate to OSP risk rating
Regular risk reporting on OSP-related customer information risks
Monitoring covers processing, storage, and disposal activities
commonGaps: No consolidated register of OSPs with access to customer information
OSP risks are not assessed or rated
Monitoring is limited to SLA performance metrics without addressing customer information risks specifically
No ongoing monitoring - only initial due diligence at onboarding
suggestedSources: Vendor management team - OSP register and risk assessments
Procurement department - OSP contracts and due diligence records
Compliance department - OSP monitoring reports

evidenceItems.name: OSP Due Diligence Assessment Reports
evidenceItems.description: Due diligence assessment reports for OSPs with access to customer information, including assessments of processing, storage, and disposal practices.
whatGoodLooksLike: Comprehensive due diligence conducted before OSP appointment
Assessment covers data processing, storage, and disposal practices
OSP's policies, procedures, and controls assessed for comparability with FSP standards
Risk assessment determines the level of ongoing monitoring required
Due diligence refreshed periodically, not just at initial appointment
commonGaps: Due diligence is limited to financial and operational assessment without customer information focus
OSP's actual control environment is not assessed (reliance on self-attestation alone)
Due diligence is conducted at onboarding but never refreshed
Assessment does not cover disposal and destruction of customer information
suggestedSources: Vendor management team - due diligence reports
